Dear !1r. Roberts:
In response to our conversation of January 5, 1990, I an
writing this letter to reiterate staff's position vith respect to
the validity of provisions of the Metropolitan water District
conflict-of-interest 
code in light of the disclosure requirernents
of the Political Reform Act (the trActx).r Thank you for your
patience in conjunction with this natter.
The specific provision at issue is contained in section
72oo(b) of your district's 
conflict-of-interest 
code. That sec-
tion provides as follows:
Except as provided in Section 7200(d)
investments in business entities and sources of
income are not reportable if the reported annual
gross income and assets of the business entity or
sources of incone each exceeded 250 nillion dollars
for the year preceding the filing of any financial
disclosure statement under this Code.
section 72oo(d) of your district's 
code provides that certain
investrnents and sources of incone are reportable in any event if
they are private hrater companies, public utilities

electric polrer to the district or companies providing tt (a)
construction services utilized in lrater activities, 
(b) electricat
equipment and supplies, and (c) engaged in real estate develop-
ment, and (d) classified as large water users.rl
section a73O2 of the Act provides, in part, as follorrs:
Each Conflict of Interest Code shaIl contain
the follorring provisions:
(a) Specific enuueration of the positions
within the agency, other than those specified in
Section 872OO, which involve the uaking or
participation in the naking of decisions which rnav
foreseeablv have a naterial effect on anv financial
interest and for each such enumerated position, the
specific types of investrnents, ... and sources of
income rrhich are reportable. An investment, ... or
source of income shall be made reportable by the
Conflict of Interest code if the business entity in
which the investrnent ... is he1d, ... or the income
or source of income may foreseeably be affected
materially by any decision made or participated in
by the designated enployee by virtue of his or her
pos ition.
(b) ... The information disclosed with
respect to reportable investrnents, interests in
real property, and income shal1 be the same as the
infonnation required by Sections 87206 and 87207.
( Enphasis added. )
Section 87302 requires that a conflict-of-interest 
code
specify the t)rpes of investnents and sources of incone which are
reportable. The filer must have received $250 or more frorn the
source of incorne and must have an investment interest of $I,000 or
more in the business entity. 
The filer nust make or participate
in naking decisions which nay foreseeably have a material effect
on the business entity which is a source of income or an invest-
ment, once it has been deternined that particular types of
investrnents and sources of incone are reportable, the Act does not
provide for linj.ting the disclosure of this infornation based upon
the annual assets or income of a business entity vhich otherwise
qualifies for disclosure.
In 1985, the Cornmission adopted Regulation lA702.1 (copy
enclosed), which provides, in part, that the effect of a decision
is rnaterial if the business entity in which the official 
has an
interest or which a source of incone to the official 
is directly
involved in a decision before the officialrs 
agency, unless the
decision will have no financial effect upon the buliness entity.
Thus, it j.s foreseeable that a business entity which has assets


.  Our Fl■e No. A-90-■56
・     Page 3
and annual incoroe in excess of $250 roilLion could be materially
affected by a decision if the entity is directly involved. This
could occur, for example, if the decision were whether to contract
lrith the entity.
Therefore, once it is deteruined that a person is a
designated ernployee subject to disclosure under a conflict of
interest code and that the business entity is a source of incoue
or investment of the type which must be disclosed, disclosure must
be made. Disclosure cannot be further linited by the size of the
business entity. 
Therefore, we again advise that the provision of
your district's 
code providing for an exemption for business enti-
ties with assets and incoDe in excess of $250 nillion be deleted.
If you have any questions regarding the above, you may
contact ne at (916) 322-59OL.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General counsel

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Metropolitan Water District's conflict-of-interest code provision exempting investments and sources of income exceeding $250 million in annual assets and gross income from disclosure requirements complies with the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the $250 million exemption provision must be deleted because once an investment or source of income is determined to be reportable under the Political Reform Act, disclosure cannot be further limited based on the size of the business entity, even if it exceeds $250 million in assets or annual income.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks explicit QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains a clear legal question and definitive FPPC position. The document is structured as a formal advice letter in response to prior conversation. File number listed as A-90-156 in header and A-90-155 on page 2 (appears to be typographical inconsistency in source document).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Metropolitan Water District's conflict-of-interest code provision exempting investments and sources of income exceeding $250 million in annual assets and gross income from disclosure requirements complies with the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advises that the $250 million exemption provision must be deleted because once an investment or source of income is determined to be reportable under the Political Reform Act, disclosure cannot be further limited based on the size of the business entity, even if it exceeds $250 million in assets or annual income.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the Metropolitan Water District regarding the validity of a conflict-of-interest code provision that exempts reportable investments and sources of income when the business entity exceeds $250 million in annual assets and gross income. The FPPC advises that this exemption violates the Political Reform Act and must be removed.
